title: What Goes On Tour

jokes.text: Murray calls a band meeting and announces their 'biggest gig ever' with dramatic buildup, then reveals it's Central Park
jokes.setup: Murray builds dramatic anticipation for their 'biggest gig ever,' Bret guesses the Grand Canyon
jokes.punchline: Murray reveals Central Park — which sounds impressive until the episode reveals it's the airport hotel lobby, and eventually a 'central park' in Newark
jokes.explanation: Works as a slow-burn plant — the Central Park reveal seems like a real win that seeds the payoff at the episode's end. Murray's managed excitement delivery is perfectly in character. Rewatch bonus is significant because viewers know the rug-pull coming.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Wouldn't have to be that big to be our biggest gig ever.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Is it the Grand Canyon one?' Murray: 'No, there's no gigs at the Grand Canyon.'
jokes.setup: Murray asks if they're ready for the big news, creating anticipation
jokes.punchline: Bret's completely left-field guess — the Grand Canyon — delivered with total sincerity, and Murray's matter-of-fact correction implying there's an ongoing history of impossible venue suggestions
jokes.explanation: Exquisitely economical. Murray's flat 'No, there's no gigs at the Grand Canyon' implies an entire history of delusional venue requests without explaining it. Bret's sincerity makes it. The follow-up 'Wouldn't have to be that big to be our biggest gig ever' doubles down beautifully on their low-stakes reality.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Headlining at Central Park? Yes. Us? Yes. Me and Bret? Yes!

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret as well?

jokes.text: Bret: 'Wouldn't have to be that big to be our biggest gig ever.'
jokes.setup: Murray's excited build-up implying the gig is enormous
jokes.punchline: Bret's cheerful acknowledgment of how pathetically small their previous gigs have been
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines in terms of pure economy. Perfectly captures the show's ethos — the band's total lack of self-pity about their failure, delivered without irony. High quotability because it functions as a standalone observation about their entire situation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've dipped into the Flight of the Conchords emergency fund.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Well, I've said a lot of things in my time, Bret. Not all of it's worth listening to, but let's just say this time around, it is.'
jokes.setup: Bret points out Murray previously said they couldn't afford a tour
jokes.punchline: Murray's non-apology apology that casually admits he often speaks without authority — an accidental confession of incompetence dressed as wisdom
jokes.explanation: Classic Murray: delivers bumbling incompetence with complete unearned authority. The self-aware admission that not all of his pronouncements are worth heeding is funnier than any direct acknowledgment of failure would be. Very strong character integration — this is Murray-specific comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Players play the game, you guys play the guitar. Don't they? They kick the ball, you kick the... jams.

jokes.text: Murray produces a mysterious wad of cash from an undisclosed 'emergency fund' — visual gag of the money appearing from nowhere
jokes.setup: Murray mentions a Flight of the Conchords emergency fund, which the band had no idea existed
jokes.punchline: He produces cash but refuses to say whose money it is or what fund it came from, and subsequent phone calls reveal he's raided his and Shelly's savings account
jokes.explanation: Functions primarily as a plant for the Shelly phone call payoffs. The visual of Murray refusing to explain the money while the band asks increasingly direct questions about it is solid character comedy. Rewatch bonus as the context of him stealing from his wife's savings retroactively makes this funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: So we have to get up at 7:00 in the morning and do 200 squat-thrusts?

jokes.text: Murray's rugby tour itinerary cross-applied to a band tour: 'Players play the game, you guys play the guitar. They kick the ball, you kick the... jams.'
jokes.setup: Band asks if Murray has organized a tour before; he admits it was a rugby tour and claims it's the same thing
jokes.punchline: 'You kick the... jams' — the analogy breaks down in real-time and Murray confidently delivers the mangled metaphor anyway
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Murray's confident pivot from 'kick the ball' to 'kick the... jams' — with the pause showing him failing in real time to complete his own analogy — is perfectly observed bureaucratic bluster. The beat where he searches for the word and lands on 'jams' is beautifully constructed. High character integration: only Murray would assert this equivalence with complete sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, you crossed out 'bus,' and you wrote 'bus.'

jokes.text: Bret asks if they have to do 200 squat-thrusts at 7am; Murray says 'some of these won't apply to you — look for the red pen'
jokes.setup: Murray has handed out itineraries that are repurposed rugby tour schedules
jokes.punchline: He's just crossed things out in red pen rather than creating a new document — the laziness of management made tangible
jokes.explanation: Good situational comedy. Murray's failure to do the basic work of creating a proper band itinerary — instead lazily annotating a rugby schedule — is a perfect emblem of his management style. Functional and consistent with the character but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'Honda Accord' was too big to fit in the gap there, so I put 'bus' back in.

jokes.text: Murray crossed out 'bus' on the itinerary and wrote 'bus' again because 'Honda Accord' was too long to fit in the gap
jokes.setup: The boys notice they aren't getting a tour bus despite the itinerary saying so
jokes.punchline: Murray's explanation: he wrote 'bus,' crossed it out, realized 'Honda Accord' wouldn't fit in the space, so wrote 'bus' again — knowing it would cause controversy
jokes.explanation: Beautifully specific comic logic. Murray's solution to the formatting problem — just writing the wrong thing back in and hoping no one notices — then adding 'I knew as soon as I wrote it there'd be some sort of controversy' shows a man who understands exactly what he's doing wrong and does it anyway. The specificity of the document-design excuse elevates this above standard incompetence comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Sometimes late at night when I'm driving alone, I imagine this is a bus.

jokes.text: Murray confesses that when driving alone at night, he imagines the Honda Accord is a bus — 'Is that a bit silly, is it, Bret?'
jokes.setup: Murray has just been forced to admit the tour vehicle is a Honda Accord
jokes.punchline: He reveals his private fantasy of pretending it's a bus, delivered with genuine vulnerability, needing Bret's validation
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. The pivot from Murray's defensive bluster to this quietly confessed fantasy is a perfect character beat — it humanizes him while being genuinely funny. 'Is that a bit silly, is it, Bret?' asks for comfort and Bret's 'No, that's cool' is the perfect straight-man response. This is only funny because of everything we know about Murray — maximum character integration. Justifies the 8+ score as it's emotionally specific, unexpected, and instantly iconic for the show.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Is that... a bit silly, is it, Bret?

jokes.text: Murray's phone call with Shelly reveals he's transferred their savings into checking to fund the tour, escalating through the call
jokes.setup: Murray assured the band the money was from an 'emergency fund'
jokes.punchline: He's drained their actual savings, keeps promising 'we will get it back' with decreasing conviction while the band watches
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe comedy — the repeated 'We will get it back' with mounting desperation is well-executed. Works as an escalation of the established financial incompetence. Not groundbreaking but solidly performed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You asked me why I was crying. I said it was the happiest day of my life. You don't remember that?

jokes.text: Murray: 'Those two effing idiots are good friends of mine, okay? ...I'm not even managing them anymore.'
jokes.setup: Murray defending the band to Shelly while they listen from the back seat
jokes.punchline: He calls them 'effing idiots' in a sentence meant to defend them, then immediately claims he's not even managing them — all with the band right there
jokes.explanation: The tension between Murray genuinely defending them while insulting them in the same breath is very characteristic of the show's affectionate-but-oblivious relationship humor. 'Good friends of mine' immediately following 'effing idiots' is the key comedic collision.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: those two effing idiots are good friends of mine, okay?

jokes.text: Murray mentions a car has been tailing them for an hour; Jemaine says 'It's probably Mel stalking us' with complete matter-of-factness
jokes.setup: Murray raises a potentially serious concern about being followed
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's flat, resigned 'It's probably Mel stalking us' — treated as the obvious and mundane explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — five words that confirm Mel's stalking is so routine it's the band's first and only hypothesis for any suspicious behavior. The deadpan is impeccable. Works brilliantly as a runner setup for her actual appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm not even managing them anymore.

jokes.text: Mel appears at the car window: 'Hey guys! What a coincidence! Ha ha!'
jokes.setup: Jemaine predicted it was Mel; she appears and pretends it's a coincidence
jokes.punchline: Mel's transparently false 'What a coincidence!' — the 'Ha ha!' is the key tell, a laugh that performs innocence while absolutely not having it
jokes.explanation: The callback payoff is instant and perfect — Jemaine literally just said 'It's probably Mel' and she appears. Her 'Ha ha!' adds just enough character specificity to elevate this from a simple callback to a genuine character moment. Mel's delusional obliviousness is at its most concentrated here.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's probably Mel stalking us.

jokes.text: The 'airport gig' is revealed to be a hotel lobby at LaGuardia — Murray introduces it as 'at the airport, in the hotel... in the lounge'
jokes.setup: They've arrived at an airport; the band is confused; Murray says 'that's where the gig is'
jokes.punchline: The venue is the hotel lobby of the airport hotel — delivered in stages as the awfulness compounds: 'at the airport' → 'in the hotel' → 'in the lounge'
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured triple-step descent. Each pause and addition ('in the hotel... in the lounge') lowers expectations one more notch. Murray's matter-of-fact delivery of escalating disappointment is pitch-perfect. The band immediately launching into 'Who likes to rock the party' without complaint captures their whole tragic dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: What a coincidence! Ha ha!

jokes.text: Bret's post-gig speech: 'Thank you very much, um, to the La Guardia Hotel lobby...' — Jemaine: 'Shut up, Bret.'
jokes.setup: The gig is over; Bret begins a sincere speech thanking the venue
jokes.punchline: He starts naming the La Guardia Hotel lobby as if it's Madison Square Garden; Jemaine's 'Shut up, Bret' is the withering reality check
jokes.explanation: The sincerity of Bret treating an airport hotel lobby gig with the gravity of a stadium concert, countered by Jemaine's flat impatience, is a great character contrast. 'Thank you very much, um, to the La Guardia Hotel lobby' is a funny line just on paper.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's where the gig is, Jemaine. It's at the airport.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Listen to the crowd.' Jemaine: 'There's hardly anyone here.' Murray: 'Yeah, it's subsided now. A lot of people have gone to get their flights.'
jokes.setup: Murray is relentlessly positive about a gig that was clearly performed to airport travelers
jokes.punchline: His explanation for the absent 'crowd' — they had flights to catch — delivered as if this is perfectly normal post-gig behavior
jokes.explanation: Murray's spin on an empty room by logically explaining why the 'audience' left — they had planes to catch — is excellent character comedy. His total unbroken positivity in the face of abject failure is the show's signature Murray move, and this is one of its better expressions.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Shut up, Bret. Don't do your embarrassing speeches.

jokes.text: Jemaine has spent his entire week's per diem on a leather suit
jokes.setup: Murray doles out per diems with explicit instructions they're for food and necessary items
jokes.punchline: Jemaine has already spent it on a leather suit — and immediately asks for an advance on tomorrow's
jokes.explanation: Solid rock-star-aspiration gag. Jemaine's immediate translation of 'food money' into 'leather suit' captures his chronic prioritization of image over survival. The immediate ask for an advance lands the escalation cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, it's only the middle of the afternoon, though.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Per diem means for the day.' Jemaine: 'Well, I don't know Latin.' Murray: 'Well, you should have said... per weekem would be the correct term.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to explain that 'per diem' means per day, chiding Jemaine for spending a week's worth
jokes.punchline: 'Per weekem would be the correct term' — Murray inventing Latin to win an argument he's already lost the point of
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Murray's pivot from correcting Jemaine's Latin ignorance to immediately inventing fake Latin ('per weekem') to win the argument is a perfect encapsulation of his character: bureaucratic authority exercised in complete ignorance. The confidence of the delivery is everything. Justifies 8+ — comparable in quality to 'kick the jams' but sharper wordplay. Highly quotable standalone line.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's hardly anyone here.

jokes.text: Bret asking for 'some of the chips that you said Bret could have' — the chip negotiation chain
jokes.setup: Murray offers Bret some chips; Jemaine wants chips; Murray redirects to Bret; Bret offers his chips; Murray declines
jokes.punchline: The complete dissolution of the chip economy: 'They're yours now' — all over approximately three chips
jokes.explanation: Functional small-scale comedy about petty negotiations among broke people. The joke slightly loses its shape across too many beats but the final 'they're yours now' has a good deflated rhythm. Below the episode's best work.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: A lot of people have gone to get their flights

jokes.text: Murray: 'I'm so livid with you turkeys. You're like a couple of cool-looking idiots.'
jokes.setup: Murray is furious about the leather suit purchase
jokes.punchline: 'Cool-looking idiots' is a perfect accidental compliment — Murray means it as pure insult but the 'cool-looking' undercuts the anger
jokes.explanation: Murray's insults that accidentally validate are a recurring comic mode for the character. 'Cool-looking idiots' is among the better ones — the rhythm is right, the unintended compliment is clear, and it's delivered in a fury that makes the slip funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Not leather suits. It's a total waste of money.

jokes.text: Murray insisting the leather suits were 'a classic rock 'n' roll move' — but attributing the idea entirely to Jemaine, not believing Bret capable
jokes.setup: Murray tells Bret he doesn't blame him for the leather suits — then explains it's because Bret would never have thought of something that cool
jokes.punchline: 'What, don't you think I'm capable of a classic rock 'n' roll move?' Murray: 'No, you're not like that. That's Jemaine all over it.'
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy built on the established Jemaine-vs-Bret dynamic. Murray's 'exoneration' of Bret is actually a deeper insult — he's too boring and sensible to have done something that stupid and cool. The dismissal is affectionate but cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You've spent tomorrow's per diem. The per diem I gave you for was for the week.

jokes.text: Murray speculating about Jemaine's behavior: 'He won't be in it. He'll be on top...' — Bret: 'No, he's tucked in.' Murray: 'Is he?' The hotel TV antenna scene
jokes.setup: Murray tells Bret to watch Jemaine for 'crazy rock star behavior'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is described as watching TV to check the weather, tucked into bed. Cut to actual scene: he's holding the antenna in an awkward position to get reception and fighting with Bret over it
jokes.explanation: The gap between Murray's wild expectations of rock star debauchery and the reality of two men fighting over a TV antenna is the episode's core comedic engine in miniature. Murray's 'Probably looking for some thunderstorms to rock out in' vs. 'He wanted to see what the weather was going to be like' is a small gem of anticlimax.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: 'Per diem' means for the day, though. See?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know Latin.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, you should have said...'per weekem,' would be the correct term.

jokes.text: Jemaine and Bret fighting over the TV antenna to maintain reception while one is trying to watch a movie
jokes.setup: They're positioned awkwardly holding the antenna; the movie is at 'a critical point'
jokes.punchline: The mundane reality of two broke musicians' hotel room entertainment — manual antenna-holding — treated with the same seriousness as any actual crisis
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that works primarily in visual performance — the image of two grown men locked in a standoff over a TV antenna is inherently funny. The 'critical point' earnestness sells it. Moderately strong but dependent on performance for full effect.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're like a couple of cool-looking idiots.

jokes.text: Bret takes nuts from the minibar after being explicitly told not to — and the TV falls out the window
jokes.setup: Murray has explicitly banned minibar use; Bret goes for the nuts anyway; they're holding the antenna which is connected to the TV
jokes.punchline: The cause-and-effect chain: Bret opens the minibar → loses antenna grip → TV presumably falls out window → 'Oh!'
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed chain of causation. The forbidden nuts leading directly to a thrown TV — without anyone actually meaning to throw the TV — is a good physical comedy setup. The structure is clean: warning issued → warning ignored → consequence. Better as a structural joke than a verbal one.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Do not call me at work, all right? I don't want them asking silly questions as to why I'm not at work

jokes.text: Murray reading the hotel bill: '$8 can of mixed nuts... you chucked a television out the window'
jokes.setup: Murray is reviewing charges and sees the minibar item
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition of the $8 nuts leading to a thrown television — delivered with comic equivalence as if they're both equally routine line items
jokes.explanation: Good payoff. 'They must have been pretty good' about the nuts is a nice understatement. The callback structure works well — the mundane item (nuts) connected to the rock-and-roll destruction (TV out the window) as if Murray is narrating a perfectly logical chain of events.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Okay, love you too! Bye-bye!

jokes.text: Murray: 'Don't cover for him, Bret. I know how rock 'n' roll he thinks he is... Mr. Keith Moon.' — Bret: 'It wasn't Jemaine, it was me.'
jokes.setup: Murray assumes Jemaine is the rock star responsible for the TV
jokes.punchline: It was actually the mild-mannered Bret — and Murray's 'I see what you're doing' assumes Bret is covering for Jemaine rather than accepting the truth
jokes.explanation: Murray's refusal to update his worldview — Jemaine is the wild one, Bret is sensible — even when directly contradicted is a solid character comedy beat. 'Mr. Keith Moon' is a nicely specific reference. The 'I see what you're doing' dismissal of the truth is characteristic Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You must have followed him, did you?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What, don't you think I'm capable of a classic rock 'n' roll move?

jokes.text: Murray: 'Would you like some mixed nuts?' Jemaine: 'Have you got any cashews?' Murray: 'No, I picked them out.' Jemaine: 'Typical.'
jokes.setup: After berating them for buying nuts, Murray is eating the hotel's confiscated/retrieved nuts
jokes.punchline: He's picked out the cashews for himself — Jemaine's 'Typical' is the perfect one-word verdict
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes. Perfect economy: three exchanges, one word payoff. Murray lecturing them about spending money on nuts, then offering them the nuts he's confiscated (or bought) — minus the best ones — is a masterclass in hypocrisy revealed through tiny specificity. 'Typical' does enormous work in a single syllable. Justifies 8+ on economy alone — comparable to 'Never go with a hippie to a second location' in terms of maximum meaning per word.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: We're on tour now. Brings out the crazier side of someone like him.

jokes.text: Murray introduces the band to a venue: 'We're just on a warm-up tour' — venue reacts: 'These are the guys who destroyed all that equipment in Passaic!'
jokes.setup: Murray is attempting to book another gig with professional composure
jokes.punchline: Their reputation has preceded them — not as musicians, but as destroyers of equipment. The Passaic incident is a surprise reveal
jokes.explanation: The revelation that they've developed a reputation — purely for destruction rather than music — is a good subversion of the 'getting known' rock narrative. Murray's 'I paid it back!' is a nice deflated response. The Passaic detail implies an entire unseen disaster that's funnier for being unexplained.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Probably looking for some thunderstorms to rock out in.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Guys, I just need a moment alone, all right?' — reaction beat as he clearly processes the venue rejecting them
jokes.setup: The venue has just recognized them as equipment destroyers
jokes.punchline: Murray's quiet request for a moment — not an explosion of frustration, just a dignified request for solitude to compose himself
jokes.explanation: The understatement of Murray needing 'a moment alone' after a public professional humiliation is good character work. His dignity is always funniest when it's the only thing he has left.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Gotta be in exactly the right place. It's your turn to hold it, man. My arms are sore.

jokes.text: The leather suits have already 'snugged up in certain areas... and in other areas, also yes'
jokes.setup: They're wearing the leather suits they blew their per diem on
jokes.punchline: The suits are already uncomfortable in a comprehensively unspecific way — 'in certain areas... and in other areas, also yes' covers all possible discomfort zones
jokes.explanation: The phrasing 'in other areas, also yes' is the joke — it's a complete circumlocution of saying 'everywhere' that manages to be more expressive about comprehensive physical discomfort than any direct statement would be. Good deadpan construction.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: $8 can of mixed nuts. They must have been pretty good. Got so much buzz out of them that you chucked a television out the window.

jokes.text: Women bet on whether they're a band or magicians or gay — 'Looks like Becky and Kathy were wrong. And Lisa and Tawny and Tracy...'
jokes.setup: A woman approaches to settle a bet about whether they're a band — the leather suits have created ambiguity
jokes.punchline: The band/magician/gay tripartite guess — and the realization mid-sentence that 'and Lisa and Tawny and Tracy' are also wrong (they thought they were gay) is left beautifully incomplete
jokes.explanation: The three-option bet (band/magicians/gay) is a good joke structure — magicians is the funniest option because it's the most specific and absurd. The incomplete second sentence 'And Lisa and Tawny and Tracy... what was it?' letting the 'gay' implication hang is well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I know how rock 'n' roll he thinks he is... Mr. Keith Moon.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Uh, we just got two fresh waters, so—' Woman: 'We'd be delighted.' — Bret overrides Jemaine's refusal
jokes.setup: Women invite them for drinks; Jemaine attempts to decline using their water as an excuse
jokes.punchline: Bret immediately accepts over Jemaine's hedge — 'We'd be delighted' — spoken with total conviction, ignoring Jemaine entirely
jokes.explanation: The clean reversal — Jemaine making a weak excuse, Bret overriding it with complete confidence — is a nice dynamic beat. 'We'd be delighted' is slightly too formal for the context, which adds a small layer of comedy. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You're watching Jemaine with his rock 'n' roll moves, buying the leather suits, getting the mixed nuts, jumping on the bed. And you thought, 'Oh, how can I equal this?'

jokes.text: Woman asks why they're dressed the same; Jemaine asks if they're 'sexy nurses' — they're a water polo team
jokes.setup: Both parties are wearing matching outfits (leather suits / water polo uniforms)
jokes.punchline: 'Are you sexy nurses?' — the wrong guess, delivered straight, followed by the 'North Jersey University Women's Water Polo team'
jokes.explanation: Functional mirror joke — both groups are confused about the other's identical outfits. 'Sexy nurses' is a slight non-sequitur that lands on Jemaine's oblivious confidence. Not the sharpest joke in the episode but does its job.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I picked them out.

jokes.text: The water polo team chants their full initialism: 'N-J-U-W-W-P-T! Whoo!'
jokes.setup: The team introduces themselves with a cheer
jokes.punchline: The initialism is seven letters long and nearly unpronounceable as a cheer — the 'Whoo!' at the end is incongruously enthusiastic for such an unwieldy acronym
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the execution of something that absolutely doesn't work as a chant — seven consonant-heavy letters — being performed with full cheerleader energy. Rewatch bonus because it pays off again at 20:52 when they do it again outside the hotel. Absurdist with specific real-world observation about sports team naming conventions.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: These are the guys that destroyed all that equipment the other night in Passaic!

jokes.text: Water polo in New Zealand: 'Do we? Pfft. It's our national sport. Every single person plays it. Bret's mom plays it.'
jokes.setup: Women ask if they have water polo in New Zealand; the boys improvise national pride
jokes.punchline: The escalating overclaim — national sport → everyone plays it → Bret's mom plays it — the specificity of 'Bret's mom' anchors the absurdity
jokes.explanation: The escalation to 'Bret's mom plays it' as the ultimate proof of ubiquity is a lovely absurdist specificity. Both characters playing along with equal conviction makes it funnier — neither blinks. Classic FOTC deadpan co-construction.

dialogue.line: Becky thought you guys were probably just a couple of magicians, and Kathy, Lisa, Tawny and Tracy thought you guys were probably just a couple of gays.

jokes.text: The autograph exchange: 'Bret?' 'Yeah.' 'Brit? Brit?' 'Bret.' 'Brit.' 'Bret!' ... 'What's your last name?' 'Bret.' 'Bret Bret?' 'No, just joking. It's McKenzie.'
jokes.setup: Woman is getting Bret's autograph and can't hear/process the name
jokes.punchline: After the name confusion, Bret jokes his last name is also Bret — then Murray: 'Don't joke, Bret... it's confusing when you joke'
jokes.explanation: The name loop is a well-worn comedy type but executed with good deadpan patience. The payoff — 'Bret Bret?' — and Murray's 'it's confusing when you joke' (implying Bret is not a natural jokester) is the sharpest part. Character-specific in that this is exactly the kind of mild, slightly confused situation Bret generates.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Are you sexy nurses?

jokes.text: 'Throw another shrimp on the barbie.' Bret: 'That's an Australian saying...' Jemaine: 'Throw another shrimp on the barbie. Crikey! Wha!'
jokes.setup: Woman asks them to say an Australian phrase, Bret correctly identifies it as Australian not New Zealand
jokes.punchline: Jemaine immediately does it anyway — full commitment to the Australian cliché they just said wasn't theirs, including 'Crikey! Wha!'
jokes.explanation: The New Zealand/Australia confusion is a running real-world irritant for New Zealanders, and the joke perfectly captures the pragmatic capitulation — Bret resists, Jemaine just does it. 'Crikey! Wha!' is perfectly calibrated absurdist delivery. Jemaine's willingness to perform for an attractive woman overrides any national pride.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Do we? Pfft. Do we?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's... everyone plays it. It's our national sport.

jokes.text: Jemaine writing down the room number with unnecessary precision: 'Be... be tidy about it.' Woman: 'I like your 2. It's nice.'
jokes.setup: They're writing down the women's room number
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's instruction to 'be tidy about it' — treating a room number like calligraphy — and the woman's sincere compliment on Bret's '2' is a miniature absurdist exchange
jokes.explanation: 'I like your 2. It's nice.' is an unexpected and oddly charming beat — flirting through handwriting appreciation. 'Be tidy about it' is Jemaine at his most inexplicably particular. Small but the specificity of the '2' compliment makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Bret Bret? No, just joking. It's McKenzie.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'So what is water polo?' Bret: 'It's like polo but in the water.' Jemaine: 'On sea horses?'
jokes.setup: Having claimed New Zealand is a water polo nation, Jemaine doesn't know what water polo is
jokes.punchline: 'On sea horses?' — the completely logical but wrong extrapolation from 'polo in water'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The callback is devastating — having just claimed this is their national sport and everyone plays it — Jemaine doesn't know what water polo is. 'On sea horses?' is the perfect punch: internally logical (polo → horses → water → sea horses) but completely absurd. The deadpan delivery of an actually reasonable-sounding question about a patently silly concept is FOTC at its finest. Justifies 8+ as comparable in construction to the anchor examples — it's efficient, character-specific, builds on what came before, and is genuinely original in its logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Don't joke, Bret, it's confusing when you joke.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Like mermaids.' Jemaine: 'Yes. Like mermaids.' — transition into the Mermaid song
jokes.setup: They're describing the women swimming in the pool
jokes.punchline: The earnest mutual agreement that the water polo players are 'like mermaids' — delivered as a profound observation — that immediately triggers a full musical fantasy sequence
jokes.explanation: The transition joke — two men staring wistfully at swimmers and agreeing they're 'like mermaids' with complete seriousness — is a good comedic gear-shift into the song. The deadpan repetition 'Like mermaids. / Yes. Like mermaids.' has a ritual quality that works as both a joke and a song setup.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Throw another shrimp on the barbie. Crikey! Wha!

jokes.text: SONG: 'Mermaid' — the entire musical sequence as a comedic set piece
jokes.setup: Two men fantasy about the water polo players as mermaids
jokes.punchline: The song is a series of sincere, practical questions about mermaid logistics — do they have webbed hands? do they smoke seaweed? — revealing the fantasy is immediately undercut by mundane concerns
jokes.explanation: The episode's musical centerpiece and one of its strongest jokes overall. The genius of the Mermaid song is that it treats the romantic fantasy with total sincerity while the actual lyrical content is anxious practical questioning: 'Would it be weird for you if I touch your fishy half? I know it would be for me.' The 'eww' reaction to the webbed hands question, the SCUBA apparatus for lovemaking, 'Or are you an optical illusion caused by a woman sitting on a rock holding half a fish — half a sexy fish' — these are genuinely original comic observations. The song is also impeccably written as music, which deepens the comedy. Justifies 8.6 — this is exceptional musical comedy writing with high character integration (only Bret and Jemaine could earnestly worry about mermaid logistics) and genuine originality.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: On sea horses?

jokes.text: 'Is it normal for a guy to wear SCUBA apparatus when he makes love in the sea?'
jokes.setup: The song is asking romantic questions about mermaid life
jokes.punchline: The sudden extremely specific practical concern about underwater sex logistics — SCUBA gear — delivered in romantic ballad style
jokes.explanation: Equally strong to the webbed hands joke. The gap between the ballad's emotional register and the logistics of SCUBA-assisted mermaid romance is maximally absurd. 'Is it normal' implies this is a genuine anxiety — he's worried about social acceptance in mermaid society. Highly quotable and original.

dialogue.line: Mermaids song sequence

jokes.text: 'Or are you an optical illusion caused by a woman sitting on a rock holding half a fish? Half a sexy fish.'
jokes.setup: The song ends with a question about mermaid reality
jokes.punchline: 'Half a sexy fish' — the qualifier 'sexy' applied to the fish half of the mermaid, delivered as a genuine concession, is the song's best line and its conclusion
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. 'Half a sexy fish' is a masterpiece of economy. The full line — 'a woman sitting on a rock holding half a fish' — is a perfect demythologization of the mermaid legend. Then 'half a sexy fish' acknowledges that even in the debunked version, they're still attracted to it. The word 'sexy' applied to the fish half is the most specific and surprising possible word choice. This is genuinely exceptional writing — comparable to the highest-scoring anchor examples in terms of surprise, economy, and the depth of character revelation in five words. The episode's clear standout line.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Or are you an optical illusion caused by a woman sitting on a rock holding half a fish?

jokes.text: After five hours waiting, Jemaine: 'Do you think maybe... they're not coming, maybe?'
jokes.setup: They've been waiting in the hotel room for the water polo team since getting the room number
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's extremely tentative posing of the obvious — framed as a suggestion, with a 'maybe' on each end — after five hours
jokes.explanation: The double 'maybe' framing ('maybe... they're not coming, maybe?') is perfectly constructed deadpan — Jemaine unwilling to commit to even this obvious conclusion. Five hours of optimism followed by the most hedged possible statement of defeat is great character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: we've been waiting for five hours

jokes.text: Bret asking Murray for a sparkling water while Murray is asleep on a single bed in the same room
jokes.setup: The girls didn't show; the boys are bored; Murray is asleep in what is presumably a shared room
jokes.punchline: Bret's polite late-night request for a sparkling water from a sleeping man, framed with 'we really need it' and 'please'
jokes.explanation: Solid situational comedy built on the established dynamic of Bret's excessive politeness and Murray's frugality. 'We really need it' about a sparkling water is a good small absurdity. Murray's 'pour tap water and blow bubbles in it' is a nicely practical and completely unsatisfying solution.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Look, just pour some tap water and blow some bubbles in it

jokes.text: Murray: 'Pour some tap water and blow some bubbles in it.' Bret: 'It's not the same.'
jokes.setup: Murray is refusing minibar sparkling water
jokes.punchline: His alternative — blowing bubbles in tap water — delivered as a genuine equivalent, and Bret's sad 'it's not the same'
jokes.explanation: Murray's 'blow bubbles in it' is a wonderfully specific and genuinely terrible solution delivered with complete authority. Bret's mournful 'it's not the same' is the perfect response — not outraged, just sadly accurate. Good deadpan exchange with strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh no, that's my autograph.

jokes.text: Hotel bill: 'Minibar charge, 0.00... Drinks charged to the room from hotel bar, $352.'
jokes.setup: Murray is checking them out of the hotel, Bret proudly says they had nothing from the minibar
jokes.punchline: The bar tab: $352. They avoided the minibar and ran up a massive bar bill instead
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure. The minibar ban was established early and repeatedly — so the $352 bar tab as the twist is a perfect pay-off: they followed the letter of Murray's law while completely violating its spirit. 'Minibar charge, 0.00' → pause → '$352' is a near-perfect comic construction in its timing and contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: This is your doing, isn't it, Rod Stewart?

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'The waters were about $175.'
jokes.setup: Murray is processing the $352 bar bill
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's matter-of-fact attribution of $175 to water — not even alcohol — making the spending even more inexplicable
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation. After being denied sparkling water from the minibar (which would have been a dollar or two), they went to the hotel bar and spent $175 on water alone. The flat delivery of this information makes it funnier than any outrage would. Also a callback to the minibar water request — they got their sparkling water, expensively.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, they're in the car.

jokes.text: Jemaine's bar bill autograph: 'Oh no, that's my autograph.' Murray: 'Yup, same... that's what a signature is.'
jokes.setup: Murray is confirming the bar charges; the bill has a signature
jokes.punchline: Jemaine thought his signature and his autograph were different things — Murray's withering clarification that they are in fact identical
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Jemaine has a separate 'autograph' identity from his 'signature' identity — suggesting he's been treating famous-person signing as a distinct mode. 'That's what a signature is' is delivered like someone explaining something to a child. Very character-specific to Jemaine's mild rock-star delusion.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well that's what happened. The Amer... on American cars, the hand brake's on this side. Oh, I must have just adjusted the seat.

jokes.text: Murray's car rolls into the pool while he's arguing — 'Go! Move the car.' / 'Oh, flub.'
jokes.setup: Someone needs to move Murray's car; Murray throws the keys to go handle it; the keys are in the car
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, flub.' — Murray's car is rolling and his mild expletive is disproportionately understated
jokes.explanation: 'Oh, flub' as the response to watching your car roll into a pool is one of the episode's most character-specific moments. Murray's mild, almost bureaucratic language even in crisis is peak Murray. The beat where he can't process the information fast enough to prevent the catastrophe works well as physical comedy setup.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: First Jemaine with the mixed nuts, then the TV. And finally, Bret, you drive my Honda into the pool!

jokes.text: Murray explaining American handbrake placement: 'On American cars, the hand brake's on this side.' Bret: 'Oh, I must have just adjusted the seat.'
jokes.setup: Murray's car is in the pool; they're trying to understand what happened
jokes.punchline: Bret's oblivious explanation — he was adjusting the seat and accidentally disengaged the handbrake — delivered without apparent awareness that this makes it worse
jokes.explanation: The innocence of 'I must have just adjusted the seat' is good Bret characterization — his disasters are always accidental and somehow even more damaging for being so. Murray's 'Oh, very rock 'n' roll, Bret' is the sharp follow-up.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Personally, I prefer the mixed nuts caper.

jokes.text: Murray: 'First Jemaine with the mixed nuts, then the TV. And finally, Bret, you drive my Honda into the pool!' — 'Personally, I prefer the mixed nuts caper. At least that was original.'
jokes.setup: Murray is enumerating the disasters of the tour as they compound
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's aesthetic critique of the disasters — preferring the mixed nuts 'caper' for its originality — as if they're rating performances
jokes.explanation: The trio callback — nuts, TV, car — assembled as a narrative of escalating destruction is well-structured. But the real joke is Jemaine treating it as a creative retrospective: 'At least that was original.' He's critiquing the artistic merit of the disasters rather than their impact. This is peak Jemaine — aesthetic consideration in completely inappropriate contexts. Justifies 8.0 — strong character integration, excellent callback structure, and the 'original' critique is genuinely surprising.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, we all... we've all made a mistake each, so it sort of evens out.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I feel like swearing.' Bret: 'Oh Murray, you wouldn't swear at us.' Murray: 'Go fuck yourself, Bret!'
jokes.setup: Murray is quitting the band in anger, building to an emotional peak
jokes.punchline: The contrast between Bret's trusting 'you wouldn't swear at us' and Murray immediately and directly swearing — plus the very specific targeting of Bret despite Jemaine being equally culpable
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top three jokes. The structure is impeccable: Murray builds to 'I feel like swearing' (which plays as a quaint understatement), Bret makes a sincere character defense, and Murray immediately obliterates it. The shock of the swear is calibrated perfectly — it's not gratuitous, it's specifically aimed at the one person who just said he wouldn't do it. Bret is also the most innocent party, making it even funnier. Highly quotable standalone. Justifies 8.2 — the setup/payoff timing is excellent and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm so angry I feel like swearing.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh Murray, you wouldn't swear at us.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Go fuck yourself, Bret!

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Personally, I prefer the mixed nuts caper. At least that was original.' Murray: 'Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Murray is furious about the escalating tour disasters
jokes.punchline: Jemaine critiques the artistic merit of the various rock-star incidents; Murray — who bought the nuts — accepts the compliment on his originality without thinking
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Jemaine comparing the 'creative merit' of the tour disasters as if they're artistic works, then Murray reflexively saying 'Thank you' to being told the nuts caper was original — not registering that he's being thanked for accidental vandalism — is perfectly economical and character-specific. The 'Thank you' is the entire joke, arriving in two words. Earns its 8+ score: maximum funny per word, character-specific to both Jemaine's critical posture and Murray's obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: But get it wet and it snugs up, it'll shrink... shrink to fit.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, I can't even get mine off.

jokes.text: They discover they have no money for a bus after Murray quits — 'I guess there's no choice, really.'
jokes.setup: Murray has quit and driven away; they're stranded
jokes.punchline: The flat resignation — no panic, no problem-solving, just 'I guess there's no choice, really' — about being penniless and stranded
jokes.explanation: Classic FOTC equanimity in the face of disaster. Their complete acceptance of being broke and stranded is consistent with their general response to adversity — mild acknowledgment and no particular urgency. Functional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: There there there there there there.

jokes.text: Mel spotting Murray walking: 'That him? No that's just some homeless guy. Oh no, that is him! Slow down, Doug.'
jokes.setup: They're driving slowly looking for Murray on the roadside
jokes.punchline: The beat of mistaking Murray for a homeless man — then realizing it is Murray — the gap between Murray's self-image and his current state
jokes.explanation: Good deflating joke. Murray walking the roadside like a homeless person is the consequence of his grand tour dreams. The double-take ('just some homeless guy... Oh no, that is him') is a clean comic beat.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yes it is. It's a central park. But it is a central park.

jokes.text: The boys present Murray with a leather suit as a peace offering — 'It's a little big. But get it wet and it snugs up.'
jokes.setup: The boys hand Murray a mystery bag as an apology
jokes.punchline: It's a leather suit — a third one, bought with the money they didn't have — with the reassurance that wet leather 'snugs up'
jokes.explanation: The callback to the leather suits as both the problem of the episode and the solution is a well-constructed circle. That the apology gift is another instance of the exact behavior Murray was angry about is perfect — they haven't learned anything. 'Get it wet and it snugs up' as comfort about an oversized leather suit has a good absurdist specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yes it is. It's a central park. But it is a central park. I said a central park in Newark.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Yeah, I can't even get mine off.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing the leather suit fitting issues
jokes.punchline: Jemaine has been trapped in his leather suit this whole time — the 'snugged up in certain areas' from earlier was an understatement — the discomfort has progressed to entrapment
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback escalation — from 'snugged up in certain areas' to complete inability to remove the garment. Said as a completely casual aside while discussing Murray's gift. The economy of the callback is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I think I've broken a piece of them.

jokes.text: Murray: 'I've done a lot of thinking that last 40 minutes when I was walking along the side of the road like a bum. I kind of realized I'm not a very good manager.'
jokes.setup: Murray is about to reconcile with the band
jokes.punchline: His confession of managerial incompetence delivered as if it's a profound road-to-Damascus insight — arriving at the conclusion the audience reached in episode one
jokes.explanation: 'Walking along the side of the road like a bum' as the crucible of self-realization is perfectly Murray — the dramatic language for a fundamentally undramatic experience. His conclusion ('I'm not a very good manager') is obvious to everyone but is delivered as a hard-won insight. '40 minutes' is the right specific duration.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Thanks, Murray.' Murray: 'But it is.' Jemaine: '...' Murray: 'There there.' — beat — 'There there there there there there.'
jokes.setup: Murray confesses he's a bad manager; the boys offer mild consolation
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's silence after Murray confirms 'but it is' — he can't actually deny it — followed by Murray self-comforting with a chain of 'there there's that escalates to six repetitions
jokes.explanation: Excellent. The beat where Jemaine's silence confirms Murray's self-assessment is the kind of reaction beat that works purely on awkward timing. Then 'there there there there there there' — Murray consoling himself when no one else will, the phrase degrading into meaningless repetition — is a beautifully observed comedic construction. Justifies 8.0: it's character-specific (no one else would do this), surprising, and the repetition escalation to six 'theres' is a perfect comic endpoint.

jokes.text: The potato/one-potato-two attempt at a handshake ritual that no one knows — 'Potato? / One potato, two... / No, just leave it.'
jokes.setup: The reconciliation requires some physical affirmation; Murray apparently has a handshake ritual
jokes.punchline: Nobody can remember how the ritual goes; it dissolves mid-attempt; 'No, just leave it'
jokes.explanation: The failed handshake ritual — an attempted emblem of solidarity that collapses because no one actually knows it — is a good anti-sentimentality beat right when the scene could become saccharine. 'No, just leave it' is the right deflation. Good FOTC rhythm.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Did you miss me?' Bret: 'Little bit.' Murray: 'Little bit, eh? Jemaine?' Jemaine: '...' [long pause] '...Oh...'
jokes.setup: Murray is fishing for affirmation after reconciliation
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's extended pause before an extremely unenthusiastic 'Oh' — not even a yes or no, just a noise
jokes.explanation: The pause is the joke. Bret's 'little bit' at least contains words; Jemaine's response is a protracted silence followed by what can only be described as a transitional noise. The 'Oh' isn't even an answer — it's an acknowledgment that a question was asked. Maximum economy. Justifies 8.0 — the awkward silence here is perfectly constructed and the non-answer is a funnier answer than any actual answer could be.

jokes.text: The Central Park reveal: 'This isn't Central Park.' — 'Yes it is. It's a central park.' — 'I said a central park in Newark... New Jersey.'
jokes.setup: The entire episode has been building to their Central Park gig — the 'biggest gig ever'
jokes.punchline: It's a central park in Newark, New Jersey — the semantic trick revealed. Murray's 'If I'd have said it was a central park in Newark, you wouldn't have done the gig' / 'Yes, we would have' / 'Well, what are you worried about, then?' is the perfect kicker
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural climax and one of the strongest jokes in the whole episode. The Central Park deception was planted in the first minute and paid off in the last — a full episode-length misdirection. The reveal itself ('a central park in Newark') is funny, but Murray's logic — 'if I'd said Newark you wouldn't have done it' → 'yes we would have' → 'well what are you worried about then?' — is the real comedic masterpiece. Murray's rhetorical trap where he's simultaneously proved his deception was unnecessary AND that the boys have no grounds to complain is perfectly constructed. High rewatch bonus — the whole episode plays differently knowing where it's going. This is the episode's best structural joke. Justifies 8.8.

jokes.text: Murray on phone to Shelly, proudly: 'Managing a band, actually, a little band called Flight of the Conchords. Yeah yeah. 'Cause that's what I do, all right? Got it? Good.'
jokes.setup: Murray has just returned to managing after quitting; he's on the phone to what appears to be his boss
jokes.punchline: He announces his band management with enormous pride — 'that's what I do, all right?' — to someone (Shelly or his boss) who presumably thinks he's at work
jokes.explanation: Murray's pride in his management role — declared with the confidence of a man who just successfully recalled his car from a swimming pool — is good character comedy. 'A little band called Flight of the Conchords' has the right amount of false modesty covering real pride.

jokes.text: Murray's cover story to his actual employer: 'I tripped over and both of my leg... muscles have... well, they contracted and I didn't know what... no. No. I don't manage a band. It has nothing to do with a band. Honestly, it's my legs. I think I've broken a piece of them.'
jokes.setup: Murray is caught — his boss has somehow heard he's been managing a band instead of working
jokes.punchline: His improvised cover story degrades in real-time: from a leg injury → 'contracted muscles' → 'I think I've broken a piece of them' — each clause worse than the last
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest closing jokes. The medical lie's increasing implausibility — tripping over → contracted leg muscles → 'I've broken a piece of them' — captures Murray's improvisation style perfectly: starting with something coherent and degrading rapidly. 'A piece of them' is the exact wrong anatomy, the kind of thing that would convince no doctor or employer. 'There's nothing going on in the background' while clearly standing in a park with a band is the perfect accompanying line. Justifies 8.2 — character-specific, escalating, and the specificity of 'a piece of them' is inspired.

jokes.text: Murray: 'No, there's nothing going on in the background. Okay.' — clearly standing at an outdoor gig
jokes.setup: Murray is lying to his employer about his whereabouts
jokes.punchline: His denial of any background activity while standing at an outdoor park gig with a band — the implied noise and activity completely contradicting his words
jokes.explanation: Functional irony joke — works well as a beat within the phone call sequence but less impressive as a standalone. The visual context (outdoor event behind him) does the heavy lifting.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Yes. I'll be in first thing.' — defeated acceptance of returning to his day job
jokes.setup: Murray has just been proudly declaring his band management identity
jokes.punchline: He folds immediately: 'Yes. I'll be in first thing.' — the arc of the episode collapses back to his day job in four words
jokes.explanation: Perfect button for the episode. All of Murray's grandiosity — the tour, the producer identity, 'that's what I do, all right?' — collapses to 'Yes. I'll be in first thing.' The economy is excellent: four words of complete capitulation that end the episode on a note of resigned ordinariness that perfectly characterizes the show's worldview.

tmdb_overview: Using the band's "emergency fund," Murray organizes a warm-up tour in preparation for a big gig in Central Park. At an airport bar, the boys get hooked by some "mermaids" from a woman's water-polo team - nearly ruining the tour and their relationship with Murray.
